sentences.text: Yeah.
Well, this-- this kind of life lasted for-- for-- well, it seems to be like a lifetime.
But-- but it wasn't.
I don't think it was more than about six months to a year.
This was still in '41.
I was born in '35, so we're talking about six, seven years old.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] during the daily life, if you weren't killed, every so often, the the Germans or the Ukraines or the villagers would come in, and if they didn't like you, or they liked you or whatever, or they just felt nasty that day, or drunk-- you would find in those days the-- the Polacks, the villagers, most of them were-- were drunkards.
Most of them were hoodlums.
I'm not an educated man, but to the history that I know of and overhearing and that, most of the villagers down there were-- were-- they were goyim.
They didn't have the education.
So it was all religious things.
And what they were taught is strictly taught from-- from-- of hatred, and the Jews were the rich.
The Jews, we had the education, and-- and they were taught that we-- we were their enemy from life on, maybe because of Christ, or-- And those were all religious people.
You're talking about Catholic Polacks, which were the Catholic.
And you're talking the Ukraines.
So you had the Eastern and you had Roman Catholics.
So part of-- part of their enemy was the Jew.

And you had when-- in the fields, and I remember when we walked by, you had gazebos, whatever you call, you had the statue of Jesus Christ all over, and you had them-- [INAUDIBLE] and we looked at them as the enemy.
So for instance, we had my two cousins, my Uncle Sam's boys.
They were older than I am.
They used to make stuff, wagons and skis and all this.
And they were just pulling a wagon, and they-- and they were killed.
And when was that?
That was in '41.
And that was just before-- before we started ghettos.
And my mother-- my mother, who was a smart woman, and she still is.
Of course, she has had some problems.
She'd seen the writing on the wall in a lot of this.
Trochenbrod, like I said, it was a small town.
A lot of them was gone already.
But a lot of them was already killed.
Our whole city was only about 12,000 people.
That's all we had.
And I would say about 2,000 or 3,000 or so was already gone, so we were less than 10,000 people left.
And when Mom's seeing the writing on the wall, that was late '41.
I would say it was about September or August, somewhere in that time period, because it was just before the holidays.
What she decided to do is take me and my sister, [?
Fay ?], and take us over to the Polack that we had the-- we had the store there, and she-- she knew the family well.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] She was going to take us over there and see if she can hide us.
So she hooked up a couple cows and the wagon and the horses, and she took us in into the village.
And as we got down there, they yell.
They kept the stuff.
And they kept my sister and I in the village.
And then the villagers sent me back.
She was scared to-- to hold me.
We had-- the situation over down there was, for a male, for a Jewish boy, was very hard.
If a Gentile wanted to find out if you were a Jew during this whole period, it's all they had to do is ask you to drop your drawers.
And they would do that any time.
You had to drop your pants, and if you were circumcised, you were killed right there.
And it was easier for a girl.
So when that farmer-- she liked her.
She was a very good friend of the family.
But she says, I can't hold you.
I'll hold Fay.
So Fay stayed there.
And I had to go back to-- to Trochenbrod.
So she put me on a horse, and she sent me back, because she was afraid for the neighbors and everybody else, that they'd find out.
And with a girl, she could get away.
And my sister looked like a [INAUDIBLE].
So she stayed there for a while.
Meanwhile, when I got back, I think it was the following week, what they did announce in our shtetl is they-- we were divided into our city.
They took 1/4 of the town, and they pushed it over to the other 3/4.
So basically, we had three ghettos.
And they took this one section of town from the small [INAUDIBLE] that was my grandfather's section, and they pushed them back into which area of the town would have been like going into north.
And they pushed us in.
So half of those people, either they killed out or whatever, and then you had to come into the other section.

And then as when they finished, when they finished, they had killed you out, then they-- on the following day, you-- you had to go in wherever you had room, and you stayed down there, because you had curfews.
You couldn't go out after-- after it was dark.
You couldn't get out of the house.
[DRINKS]
And Mom and I, my mother would handle the children's stuff, clothes.
That's where she used to steal sometimes.
She'd steal an extra dress or something for-- for my brother.
And I used to handle shoes.
I had to go around and take the shoes off of them.
Then we threw them in mass graves.
And then they used to come around in the morning, and they would ask for volunteers, able bodies.
And they would take those able bodies and-- no.
First, before that, they would have some elders go out, and-- especially the Jewish police-- and they would dig graves on the outside of the city.
And they would come in with the trucks, and they'd pick up some people, and they would shoot them.
And what they were doing, basically, in those days is they were thinning the population.
And-- and they would kill them.
And the first-- the first group of people that were these that they killed was those Jews who [NON-ENGLISH]-- who told on on the other Jews.
They were the turncoats, the [NON-ENGLISH]-- the-- not the spies.
They-- they were the ones that were killed.
They used to get a lot of the older Jews, and they would say, you tell us where this one is, or where the gold is, or where this, and they had them-- you're going to dig the graves.
And they dig the graves, then you're going to be spared.
But they were the first ones that they shot.
They put them in there.
And then we'd go, and they would have to get dressed.
And then we would go, we'd pick up the clothes and put it  down in piles.
And well, this thing went on for about three times.
And then after a while, when the city was pretty well thinned out, they made another section.
Now you were already in a half of the city.
And this was just about coming up to the middle of the town.
And they-- they also took from one side of the street, and now they put us all on the other side of the street.
So we were already up to the shul from there.
And then they took from the other end of the town, they brought everybody towards the middle of the town.
And we went through that three times.
Well, approximately-- to the best of my knowledge, and from this-- there-- there's a lot of talk about a lot of people in there.
I don't think, out of the 12,000, I don't think we have more than 200, 300 people that are alive.
Most of my relatives from my family survived.
I don't think we had more than two-- I think I would be stretching it like to the end of its teeth if we had 250 people survive Trochenbrod.
The only ones that-- we-- we had a lot from the ghettos, we had a lot of survivors.
But then we were in the forest.
In the forest, we used to have the [INAUDIBLE], which is a different story.
But still being in the town, on the third section, they pushed everybody in, and we had to move over from the center house, and we had to move.
Mom and I and my sister and my brother, we were pushed over to a house.
And luckily, we were into a house that belonged to my cousin, my mother's aunt.
And we knew the house well.
And we stayed down there for a while.
And you used to go out every day.
You used to go out and do the work.
We still had to work.
We had to provide food and work the fields and milk the cows and-- for-- for-- to give the-- the soldiers and all them.
They were eating, and they were killing.
It was a-- it was a very rough time.
And the way they were set up, for instance, we went to-- when you get up in the morning, you go to work real early.
And our streets where was the-- was-- had posts on each side.
And there was a soldier or somebody with a rifle would sit.
And as you walk towards the graves when you did your-- finished your work, when you went there, and you went back, you had to go from one to other ones, like zigzag in the street.
And they would all [INAUDIBLE] you.
And the women was-- men were not hurt as bad as most of the women.
They used to take the kids and the women, and they used to rape them right down there or else kill them or cut them apart.
This-- the-- the-- the-- the-- more vicious harm they could do, these young Ukraines, I mean, the more they were rewarded.
It was just plain simple.
So we went-- we went through like that for what seemed to be years, but it wasn't much.

And when the town was real [INAUDIBLE], on the last wave, instead of taking us, they-- they came up with truckloads of people, and they started taking out the-- first they asked for anybody that can walk by themselves.
And women that held children on their hands were not going to go.
So my mother picked up my brother, and she was holding him.
And then Fay were assigned.
We were already sent out.
And those-- those German trucks, they were kind of high.
But you had the-- the-- the canvas covers on it.
And we went up on one side, and we sneaked out, and we went in through-- we went in on the one side of the truck, Fay and I, and she says, I'm not going.
And I said, OK.
We went up.
And she says, they're going to kill us anyway, so let's jump.
Because we knew-- at that time, we already knew what's going on, because we worked.
And the word was out, when they said they'd take you to work, we knew what the work meant.
And this was already the fourth time.
So the alternative or this was about the same.
Ah.
I'd say it's-- all I know, it was early in the morning.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Days in those days did not count as days.
The season was late, late summer.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We didn't know from days.
We didn't know from-- we only knew is-- when it's light and darkness.
And most the time, in the hiding, it was darkness 24 hours a day, because you were hiding.
So-- so for people like, like me and I guess thousands of us, days and-- didn't mean anything.
We-- it just meant surviving.
And if you went out, and you're still alive, you were breathing, you knew you survived another.
But when we jumped off of the-- of that truck, Fay and I, but that time, we went in through the back door.
We knew the house well, because we used to go over there all the time for Shabbos and play.
So it was hardly anybody left in that home.
So when we came back in, and we met Mom down there, and she says, we've got to hide.
This is going be the last one.
Because the city was already thinned out.
There was only maybe 10, 12 houses left with people, maybe-- yeah.
That house.
It was about-- that's about all there was.
So she says, we've got to hide.
And she knew-- like I said, we knew the house real good.
And what Mom did is she opened up the cellar, where the-- the-- the people was making hide.
And we used to do our own shoe leathers and make hide.
You put it in lime, and you let the hair come off, and you process the shoes.
Because further up in the [INAUDIBLE] on this, it's like Columbus and Whitehall for this.
You know, that was the whole-- that section of town, like Whitehall here, was where they produced and made a lot of leather.
Matter of fact, Columbus has a lot of those people.
All the Schwartzes, you know, the-- the Schwartzes, they come from Trochenbrod.
A lot of the-- a lot of my family settled in Columbus.
They were the [NON-ENGLISH].
That's where the hides and furs was made and processed for all around that area.
And we were sort of, quote, 'bootleg' hide makers, as part from, because you had all this cattle.
So there was-- when we-- Mother opened up the cellar, there was already two people sitting there, and there was room for two barrels, and then you had-- in the sides, you know how a barrel is round.
And there were two people down there, and there was only room for one person.
So my sister-- she put my sister in there.
And the only place I could-- and I-- I still scream about it whenever I think about it-- and she threw me in a barrel of-- of lime, and she covered us back up.
She put a table on top of it.
And then she and my brother hid under-- hid in the cellar.
They hid in a closet what they had.
And that was under the foundation.
There was-- right near the foundation, you tear-- you put a-- most of them were dirt floors.
And she dug it out, and then she climbed down underneath there, and that's where they hid, there.
So Mom and my brother was hidden there.
So we laid down there, it seemed like eternity, but my sister and I and the other two people were-- no, actually, three people.
There were three people there and my sister, and there were five of us.
And we laid down there three days and four nights, yeah, because it was early in the morning.
It was-- then and four nights, we laid there.
And my mother was with my brother there.
And the only thing we had for all of us was one potato.
And then that's all we lived on.
We got up, then I was-- my skin, it was just peeling, and I was crying.
I got out one time, middle of the night and tried to get up, and there Mom just threw me back in there.
She says, you're going to die anyhow, so you might as well stay there.
I still get hives when I-- when I talk about it.
I laid in there.
And when I got out, I was raw.
I was completely raw.
I couldn't move.
And the-- what happened was on the-- on the fifth day-- yeah.
It was a night, actually.
Mom came over, and she says, let's go, kids.
We're going to-- we're going to run away.
We're going to run out to the woods.
Well, the forest in our town was close to-- to that side of the street, because we used to go pick blueberries, [INAUDIBLE], it was on that side.
And on our side of the street is where you had the fields.
That side of the street was forest, just forest, and I'm talking about it used to be thick.
And we ran away that night, Mom and my brother and my sister and I, we ran into the forest, and we hid.
And it was a good thing, because the next day, the-- everything went out, and we were still in the forest.
Now--
Everything was burned out.
All of the-- all of the people were already killed out.
Anybody who could.
And they had an uprising when they-- when they were killing some of the-- at the-- at the-- at the shooting grounds.
The shooting grounds consisted of where they-- they dug a big grave, round grave.
And the people would line up, get undressed.
They would line up.
And it would-- and you would fall head first-- you know, you still-- you were all heads.
It had different layers.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] on-- on the top, they'd put in just a little bit of dirt.
I-- I still-- I can still visualize that.
[DRINKS] Because when we-- when we went to take the clothes, you could still see the graves move or steam come out.
So it had to  be in the fall.
Steam come out from the graves.
And we had the policemen staying there.
And they had the guns.
And they had-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But anyhow, so we ran away.
And my family knew that [INAUDIBLE] especially.
And there was sort of an unwritten rule that if and when the Cossacks, well, if something happens, we all meet in this one place.
So-- in this one forest near [INAUDIBLE] Polack.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So when we escape, we ran-- we ran into the woods.
And we stayed down there for a couple days.
And then we were trying to get back to the forest that we met.
And Mom took us there.
And when we got into that forest, we met up with my zeyde.
My mother's father.
We met up with him.
And a couple days later, my uncle, Uncle Ben, he came down with his daughter.
She was Rita, my cousin.
And then I had my aunt-- my aunt and her daughter came over there.
Well, they came in-- we knew the forest.
And once you got in the forest, we knew more or less that outside this, outside in this section, if something happens, we've got to stay there until we meet, or else we give up.
So when you tell the story, it sounds like this.

And after a while, my uncle Sam, my uncle Ben, and-- and my cousin and her mother, Sarah-- they live in Boston-- and Mom and I, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we met right down there, and we joined the group, and we went over to deeper in the forest.
And this was still in [INAUDIBLE].
This was in the village.
It was-- the-- the group was my mother, my zeyde, my two uncles, my aunt, and then I had two cousins, two female cousins, and then my brother and my sister and I.
So five kids and five adults, basically, is who it was.
My uncle Sam was all by himself, because his two boys and his wife was already killed.
And my uncle Ben, which is Rita's-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] his wife and boy were already killed in Lutsk.
And he only grabbed his daughter Rita and ran over there to meet us.
And my grandmother and my aunt, my mother's sister, the younger one, they were killed in the-- in the barn in the house on the way to the ghetto.
They were shot there.
And matter of fact, my grandmother was shot with a cow, which is right on top of her.
We seen that.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Anyhow, you know, sometimes, I-- I said when-- [DRINKS]
And we were in this one forest.
We had a big sweep, the Germans and the Polacks and the dogs and everybody, they-- they surrounded the forest.
And we lived down there for a few months, sort of peaceful.
Everybody was--
They catch.
And they caught-- they caught most of it.
But my zeyde, my-- my grandfather, he was a survivor.
He knew.
And my mother, they had this sort of a gut feelings, or fifth sense, or ninth sense, whatever you want to call it.
And a few days-- a day or so before that, before the big sweep, they said, there's too many Jews in this-- [YIDDISH] We used to speak Yiddish, basically, still do.
And most of the stuff that comes to me was in Yiddish, because the talking, the thinking, and a lot of I interpret it first.
I think of what they said in Yiddish, and then I interpret it over.
But they basically what they said is, this is no good.
We've got to go.
We got to get split up, because we're going to-- so we ran away from that forest, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] actually, 9, 10 of us.
And we went into this other forest.
And they had a big sweep in there.
And everybody that I know of-- there was still quite a few Jews.
There must have been at least 100, 200 Jews in there that survived the-- the final ghetto.
But they were all killed, most of them.
Because we went into this other forest, and we met up form some that were in this different [NON-ENGLISH], but from-- but relatives.
And that clan that survived lives in Cleveland now, [INAUDIBLE] and his clan.
We met them down there.
Now, life in the forest was simple.
You had to live like an animal, think like an animal, and try to survive off of the land.
Things that you learn when you're young.
And we had an unusual situation, where we had me, who was the oldest boy, and my uncle Ben, and Zeyde.
My mother was-- was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my-- my mother was always preoccupied with my brother.
And life in those days-- it's hard to believe now, but life in those days were very, very-- oh, it didn't have much meaning.
Surviving was the-- was the agenda, and no matter how-- and that means that if-- if it's-- it's either you or I, and if I have a better chance to survive than you, then so be it.
I'll give you an example.
My uncle, which was a very nice man.
He would give you his right hand.
But there were-- my-- my young brother, and so all he wanted was a drink of water.
And when you go around for weeks, and we didn't have anything.
And we were running away from this attack.
And he kept on-- he was the only one who was crying so much.
So my uncle grabbed him and took off his belt and hung him.
He still has a mark, my brother.
And my mother grabbed him, took him off.
And she actually urinated and she gave him to drink, and he became quiet.
And she saved his life.
But this is the desperation that I'm trying to bring out that we had.
But we were very close.
So it was me and my zeyde was pretty much in charge of supplying the food or searching and getting.
Zeyde knew where to get it.
He knew all the farms.
Day or night, he could sneak into different places, because he lived all his life around there.
And me, I was the only one, the strongest, and the only one capable of doing anything.
My other-- my uncle took-- had to stay take care of the-- of the women and the kids.
And my-- my older uncle, he was pretty much shot down.
He just didn't-- he didn't have any life in him to-- why should I?
My kids, my wife is dead, and I don't have anything.
Why should I suffer?
And he didn't want to partake in too much of it.
So what we did for about two years, two and a half years, if not more, my zeyde and I used to go out and search.
At night, we used to search for food.
For example, we used to go out to the farms, and we would get eggs.
And this lays in my mind real good.
We used to go steal eggs at the farms.
In Europe, they used to have on top of the-- in the barn on top, in the hay, they used to store their eggs for-- for the winter time.
So we used to take a little pinhole, make an egg, suck it out, and then put it in the little cup.
And you can-- you don't need bigger than a pinhole, you can suck an egg out.
And we used to suck it out and then spit it back out in a cup, and we'd bring home a few eggs.

In the summer and the fall, when you go to peel-- pick potatoes, for example, we would never pick more than one or two potatoes in one-- I don't know if you've ever seen in a garden, but potatoes grow in patches.
Sometimes they'll have 10, 12, or 6 potatoes on the one root.
But we'd never take more than one or two potatoes out of one root, and never-- never from one-- from one [INAUDIBLE] to the next.
But you would skip rows and get around, so they wouldn't trace you.
